hints.to become: 'to become' is 'fieri' — the passive of 'facere', but 'fecerunt' is active

hints.Present indicative: Present would be 'faciunt' — 'fecerunt' with perfect stem 'fec-' is perfect
hints.Future indicative: Future would be 'facient' — 'fecerunt' is perfect indicative
hints.Pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'fecerant' — 'fecerunt' with -erunt is perfect

hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry) — 'fecerunt' comes from 'facere'
hints.fio, fieri, factus sum: These are the parts of 'fieri' (to become) — 'fecerunt' is active 'facere'
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are the parts of 'fugere' (to flee) — a different verb

hints.Accusative: Accusative plural of 3rd declension also ends in -es, but 'amores' is the subject of 'fecerunt'
hints.Genitive: Genitive singular would be 'amoris' — 'amores' is nominative plural
hints.Vocative: This is a statement, not an address — 'amores' is nominative subject

hints.1st: 1st declension plural nominative ends in -ae — 'amor, amoris' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension plural nominative ends in -i — 'amor' is 3rd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension plural nominative ends in -us — 'amor, amoris' is 3rd declension

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural of 3rd declension also ends in -es, but 'civitates' is the direct object of 'fecerunt'
hints.Ablative: Ablative plural would be 'civitatibus' — 'civitates' is accusative
hints.Genitive: Genitive singular would be 'civitatis' — 'civitates' is accusative plural

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'civitas, civitatis' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'civitas' with -tas suffix is 3rd declension
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'civitas, civitatis' is 3rd declension

steps.prompt: What two cities does Augustine describe?
steps.target: The City of God (amor Dei) and the Earthly City (amor sui)
choices: The City of God (amor Dei) and the Earthly City (amor sui)

hints.Rome and Jerusalem: Augustine's 'two cities' are theological allegories, not literal cities
hints.Heaven and Hell: The two cities are defined by love (of God vs. of self), not by afterlife destinations
hints.Athens and Sparta: Augustine is not discussing Greek city-states — his cities are defined by opposing loves
